audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:; 4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.; Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.; Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.; Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.; Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin... | Audition: In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:; 4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.; Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.; Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.; Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.; Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Bassoon.; Two contrasting etudes from Weissenborn, Milde, or similar study books; Two contrasting solo works or movements from a sonata or concerto; The graduate repertoire in bassoon is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.; In addition to those general requirements, graduate students should prepare the following:; Several contrasting orchestral excerpts of the applicants’ choice; Two contrasting solo works; You are urged to perform repertoire which best represents your performance level and musical taste.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.; Visit Peabody; Directory; Friedheim Music Library; Johns Hopkins University; Clery Act; Privacy; Peabody Institute of The Johns Hopkins University 1 East Mount Vernon Place Baltimore, MD 21202; 667-208-6500; Facebook | Audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Bassoon.
Two contrasting etudes from Weissenborn, Milde, or similar study books
Two contrasting solo works or movements from a sonata or concerto

The graduate repertoire in bassoon is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
In addition to those general requirements, graduate students should prepare the following:
Several contrasting orchestral excerpts of the applicants’ choice
Two contrasting solo works

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Learn more about Pre-Screening
A movement of a concerto from standard literature or 5-10 minutes of an equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
Prelude plus one other movement from an unaccompanied suite by Bach.
One etude from one of the following: Duport 21 Etudes, Popper High School of Cello Playing, Piatti 12 Caprices
Two contrasting movements of a concerto from standard literature or equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
A prelude plus one other movement from an unaccompanied suite by Bach.
A shorter piece of your choice or a complete movement from a sonata or larger work, which shows a stylistic or personal musical interest.
Complete major concerto from the romantic period to the present, or equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
1st movement of Haydn Concerto in C or D, including cadenza.
A complete unaccompanied suite by Bach.
Choice of any complete sonata or solo piece.
Any additional work from the late 20th or 21st century.

AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. To meet this requirement, please follow the repertoire outline for the DMA listed above.
If, after passing the pre-screen, an invited applicant is unable to attend the auditions in person and qualify for a recorded audition, they may follow these guidelines for a recorded audition:
Please submit the Bach requirements in their entirety (omitting repeats in the dance movements)
audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. To meet this requirement, please follow the repertoire outline for the DMA listed above.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Undergraduate; Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.; Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.; Graduate; Two arias and a song (one in English) is required. | Audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800; A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included; One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_; One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_; Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.; Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Undergraduate
Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.
Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Learn more about Pre-Screening
A movement of a concerto from standard literature or 5-10 minutes of an equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
Prelude plus one other movement from an unaccompanied suite by Bach.
One etude from one of the following: Duport 21 Etudes, Popper High School of Cello Playing, Piatti 12 Caprices
Two contrasting movements of a concerto from standard literature or equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
A prelude plus one other movement from an unaccompanied suite by Bach.
A shorter piece of your choice or a complete movement from a sonata or larger work, which shows a stylistic or personal musical interest.
Complete major concerto from the romantic period to the present, or equivalent piece such as Schelomo or Rococo Variations.
1st movement of Haydn Concerto in C or D, including cadenza.
A complete unaccompanied suite by Bach.
Choice of any complete sonata or solo piece.
Any additional work from the late 20th or 21st century.

AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. To meet this requirement, please follow the repertoire outline for the DMA listed above.
If, after passing the pre-screen, an invited applicant is unable to attend the auditions in person and qualify for a recorded audition, they may follow these guidelines for a recorded audition:
Please submit the Bach requirements in their entirety (omitting repeats in the dance movements)

A prelude plus one other movement from an unaccompanied suite by Bach.

prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st
If you pass the pre-screening, you will be invited to audition with an ensemble at Peabody.
2 performance excerpts, each 8-10 minutes in length, of contrasting repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
1 rehearsal excerpt, 5 minutes total in length.
3-4 excerpts, each 2-3 minutes in length, of contrasting standard repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
A full-size orchestra is not required; a quintet with piano would suffice, for example.
Appropriate dress is expected.
Selections from the standard Wind Ensemble/Chamber Winds repertoire is preferred.
The conductor should be recorded from the front so that their eyes, face, hands, gestures and posture are all clearly seen. Please no videos filmed from the conductor’s back.
Total length of video**: 15-20 minutes recommended; 30 minutes maximum.
The subject should be shown rehearsing for part of the video and performing for the remainder.
Suggested time allocation: 1/2 rehearsal, 1/2 performance. The performance component should be just that, a complete run of a composition or movement.
The chosen excerpts should include a variety of musical styles as well as some technically challenging passages.
The conductor’s name, the repertoire, the date, and location of the selected rehearsals and performances, and the ensembles conducted.
A brief description of the type of ensemble (e.g. student, community, semi-professional, workshop, etc.)
For the rehearsal portion of the recording, please include where the recording was made in the rehearsal sequence. For example, this was the third rehearsal in a sequence of six rehearsals.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
audition: Session with a lab choir, to be conducted from the full score, _not_ a piano/vocal score
MM/GPD – Ethel Smyth / J.S. Bach: _Five Sacred Partsongs,_ No. 1 “Komm, süßer Tod” and Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_
DMA –Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_ and Stravinsky: _Symphony of Psalms,_ mvmt I
Interview/Keyboard skills assessment with Beth Willer, Director of Choral Studies
Score analysis
Regular Decision:
Schumann:_Symphony No. 2_
Expositions of all movements
Copland: _Appalachian Spring_(original 13-instrument version)
2 before #35-51
Beethoven:_Symphony No. 5_
Late Admission:
At least two contrasting excerpts from the standard repertoire
Examples include Brahms: _Symphony No. 4_, Mozart: _The Magic Flute_, Copland:_Appalachian Spring Suite_(Orchestral version)
Interview: Stravinsky, _Octet for Wind Instruments_
Audition with the Peabody Wind Ensemble: Johan de Meij,_Symphony No. 1, Mvt. 4 “Journey in the Dark”_

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Clarinet.
Mozart Concerto in A major, K. 622, slow movement
and one of the following:
Weber Concerto No 1 in F minor, 1st or 3rd movement
Weber Concerto No. 2 in E-flat major, 1st or 3rd movement
Mozart Concerto in A major, K. 622, 1st or 3rd movement
Weber Concertino, Op 26
May include any standard repertoire piece which best represents your performance level and technical proficiency

The graduate repertoire in clarinet is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
In addition to those general requirements, graduate students should include several contrasting orchestral excerpts of the applicants’ choice.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Clarinet.
Mozart Concerto in A major, K. 622, slow movement
and one of the following:
Weber Concerto No 1 in F minor, 1st or 3rd movement
Weber Concerto No. 2 in E-flat major, 1st or 3rd movement
Mozart Concerto in A major, K. 622, 1st or 3rd movement
Weber Concertino, Op 26
May include any standard repertoire piece which best represents your performance level and technical proficiency

The graduate repertoire in clarinet is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
In addition to those general requirements, graduate students should include several contrasting orchestral excerpts of the applicants’ choice.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st; Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.; Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.; Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:; Two octave major scale; Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor); Two octave Dorian minor; Two octave harmonic minor; Each of the corresponding arpeggios; Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.; Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:; How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.; What interests you in Peabody Jazz?; If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here? | Audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.; At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:; Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:; A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.; A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.; A standard ballad.; Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).; Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.; Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.; Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.; In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:; 4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.; Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.; Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.; Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.; Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Scores consisting of a variety of instrumentations are strongly encouraged, but not required.
An orchestral score is not required.
Please be sure to clearly indicate the names of the compositions.
For the recordings, acoustic performances are strongly encouraged though MIDI performances are acceptable.
Large ensemble works are welcome, but not required.
Instrumental proficiency is not required, but strongly encouraged.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Prospective students should submit their best three (3) pieces as part of the application.
Please be sure to clearly indicate the titles of each composition.
For the recordings, acoustic performances are strongly encouraged though MIDI performances are acceptable if necessary.
Prospective MM students who wish to audition are expected to demonstrate a solid overview of contemporary music, including music written since 1945.
Prospective students should submit their best three (3) pieces as part of the application, representing as wide a variety of scoring/instrumentation as possible.
Prospective DMA students who wish to audition are expected to demonstrate their ability to be conversant in all aspects of contemporary music through the present-day.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Early Decision (BM only)**: November 1st; Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; Note: The application materials listed below will be considered as pre-screening for all Music for New Media applicants. The faculty will review these materials to determine whether or not the applicant will be offered a final interview.; Learn more about Pre-Screening; Have a basic knowledge of music notation and the rudiments of music theory; Possess, or be willing to learn, a working knowledge of any MIDI sequencer and/or Digital Audio Workstation application (we teach with Logic Pro X and ProTools); Show strong academic achievement as demonstrated through GPA; Basic understanding of music and application of musical skills; Enthusiasm for film and game music, and the ability to differentiate the field from pop and electronic music; Able to discuss varying emotional states in dramatic situations; Bachelor’s degree in music or equivalent study; Understanding of music and application of musical skills; Enthusiasm for film and games and their music; Ables to discuss a variety of emotional states in dramatic situations; Submit a portfolio of at least five original works, demonstrating creativity and an understanding of the fundamentals of composing music with traditional or software instruments.; You may submit as many pieces as you like, but be sure to include at least _three_ pieces that are _not_ “beat-driven” or loop-based; these should be instrumental pieces, and can range from ambient synth beds, to solo piano or full orchestrations; they should be designed to set a mood or enhance drama and emotions, as appropriate for film, TV or game soundtracks.; These can be audio or video files (.WAV, .MOV, .MP3, etc.) of any length – even a 16-bar piece is admissible if it demonstrates melody, phrasing, harmony, instrumentation, and mood. It is acceptable to include “rescores” i.e. video clips from Hollywood shows or games, substituting the original soundtrack with your own music.

prescreen: Early Decision (BM only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st

Note: The application materials listed below will be considered as pre-screening for all Music for New Media applicants. The faculty will review these materials to determine whether or not the applicant will be offered a final interview.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Have a basic knowledge of music notation and the rudiments of music theory
Possess, or be willing to learn, a working knowledge of any MIDI sequencer and/or Digital Audio Workstation application (we teach with Logic Pro X and ProTools)
Show strong academic achievement as demonstrated through GPA
Basic understanding of music and application of musical skills
Enthusiasm for film and game music, and the ability to differentiate the field from pop and electronic music
Able to discuss varying emotional states in dramatic situations
Bachelor’s degree in music or equivalent study
Understanding of music and application of musical skills
Enthusiasm for film and games and their music
Ables to discuss a variety of emotional states in dramatic situations
Submit a portfolio of at least five original works, demonstrating creativity and an understanding of the fundamentals of composing music with traditional or software instruments.

You may submit as many pieces as you like, but be sure to include at least _three_ pieces that are _not_ “beat-driven” or loop-based; these should be instrumental pieces, and can range from ambient synth beds, to solo piano or full orchestrations; they should be designed to set a mood or enhance drama and emotions, as appropriate for film, TV or game soundtracks.

These can be audio or video files (.WAV, .MOV, .MP3, etc.) of any length – even a 16-bar piece is admissible if it demonstrates melody, phrasing, harmony, instrumentation, and mood. It is acceptable to include “rescores” i.e. video clips from Hollywood shows or games, substituting the original soundtrack with your own music.

It is acceptable to include “rescores” i.e. video clips from Hollywood shows or games, substituting the original soundtrack with your own music.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
One movement from any of the Solo Suites for cello by Johann Sebastian Bach
One movement of a standard concerto or equivalent solo work
One orchestral excerpt
One movement from a standard concerto
Three orchestral excerpts of contrasting styles
Three solo works of contrasting styles
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Two contrasting pieces from the standard repertoire
Individual movements of sonatas, concertos, or suites are acceptable.
Two contrasting orchestral excerpts
Three contrasting pieces from the standard repertoire, including at least one selection from the solo cello suites of Johann Sebastian Bach
Four contrasting orchestral excerpts
The DMA repertoire in double bass should mirror a typical graduate recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles. Applicants should submit the following:
At least three movements from the same solo Bach Cello Suite (no repeats)
First two movements from a standard Double Bass concerto (with cadenzas where applicable)
audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. To meet this requirement please include:
Three movements from a single solo Bach Cello Suite (no repeats)
First two movements from a standard Double Bass concerto (with cadenzas where applicable)
One contemporary piece for Double Bass written after 1950
One showpiece
A transcription of a piece originally written for another instrument is acceptable
Please limit to 10 minutes or less

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st; Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application; Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; All candidates must perform/record with a pianist (with the exception of the solo unaccompanied piece and the excerpts).; The pre-screening video must include:; Two contrasting movements from a sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or two contrasting movements from either Mozart Concerto KV 313 or KV 314 (must include cadenzas); A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute from any period; A third contrasting complete work of the candidate’s choice (typically a work for flute and piano such as a sonata, a concerto, or from the French school); Two orchestral excerpts:; Mendelssohn: Scherzo from A Midsummer Night’s Dream (2 measures before P to the end); Beethoven: Leonore Overture No. 3 (mm. 328-360); _Please note that etudes are not considered acceptable audition repertoire._; Learn more about Pre-Screening; A complete sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or a complete concerto by Mozart (including cadenzas); A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute; 3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice; Sight-reading may be requested at the audition | Audition: A program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including:; At least two large-scale works which best reflects the advanced standard of the candidate; One work written after 1975; One unaccompanied solo piece; 3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice; Sight-reading may be requested at the audition

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
All candidates must perform/record with a pianist (with the exception of the solo unaccompanied piece and the excerpts).
The pre-screening video must include:
Two contrasting movements from a sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or two contrasting movements from either Mozart Concerto KV 313 or KV 314 (must include cadenzas)
A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute from any period
A third contrasting complete work of the candidate’s choice (typically a work for flute and piano such as a sonata, a concerto, or from the French school)
Two orchestral excerpts:
Mendelssohn: Scherzo from A Midsummer Night’s Dream (2 measures before P to the end)
Beethoven: Leonore Overture No. 3 (mm. 328-360)
_Please note that etudes are not considered acceptable audition repertoire._
Learn more about Pre-Screening
A complete sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or a complete concerto by Mozart (including cadenzas)
A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute
3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice
Sight-reading may be requested at the audition
audition: A program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including:
At least two large-scale works which best reflects the advanced standard of the candidate
One work written after 1975
One unaccompanied solo piece
3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice
Sight-reading may be requested at the audition

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Undergraduate; Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.; Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.; Graduate; Two arias and a song (one in English) is required. | Audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800; A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included; One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_; One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_; Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.; Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Undergraduate
Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.
Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st; Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application; Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; All candidates must perform/record with a pianist (with the exception of the solo unaccompanied piece and the excerpts).; The pre-screening video must include:; Two contrasting movements from a sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or two contrasting movements from either Mozart Concerto KV 313 or KV 314 (must include cadenzas); A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute from any period; A third contrasting complete work of the candidate’s choice (typically a work for flute and piano such as a sonata, a concerto, or from the French school); Two orchestral excerpts:; Mendelssohn: Scherzo from A Midsummer Night’s Dream (2 measures before P to the end); Beethoven: Leonore Overture No. 3 (mm. 328-360); _Please note that etudes are not considered acceptable audition repertoire._; Learn more about Pre-Screening; A complete sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or a complete concerto by Mozart (including cadenzas); A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute; 3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice; Sight-reading may be requested at the audition | Audition: A program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including:; At least two large-scale works which best reflects the advanced standard of the candidate; One work written after 1975; One unaccompanied solo piece; 3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice; Sight-reading may be requested at the audition

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
All candidates must perform/record with a pianist (with the exception of the solo unaccompanied piece and the excerpts).
The pre-screening video must include:
Two contrasting movements from a sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or two contrasting movements from either Mozart Concerto KV 313 or KV 314 (must include cadenzas)
A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute from any period
A third contrasting complete work of the candidate’s choice (typically a work for flute and piano such as a sonata, a concerto, or from the French school)
Two orchestral excerpts:
Mendelssohn: Scherzo from A Midsummer Night’s Dream (2 measures before P to the end)
Beethoven: Leonore Overture No. 3 (mm. 328-360)
_Please note that etudes are not considered acceptable audition repertoire._
Learn more about Pre-Screening
A complete sonata by Johann Sebastian Bach or a complete concerto by Mozart (including cadenzas)
A complete unaccompanied work for solo flute
3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice
Sight-reading may be requested at the audition
audition: A program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including:
At least two large-scale works which best reflects the advanced standard of the candidate
One work written after 1975
One unaccompanied solo piece
3 contrasting orchestral excerpts of the candidate’s choice
Sight-reading may be requested at the audition

Applicants should prepare a recital program demonstrating significant performance skills. The program should be memorized, at least 35 minutes long, and showcase the applicant’s technical ability and interpretative skills. Works chosen should represent a variety of historical styles.

Applicants should prepare a recital program demonstrating advanced musical and technical ability. The program should be no less than 60 minutes long, represent at least three style periods, and include at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Undergraduate; Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.; Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.; Graduate; Two arias and a song (one in English) is required. | Audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800; A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included; One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_; One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_; Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.; Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Undergraduate
Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.
Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Horn.; Exposition of the first movement of a concerto by Mozart or Richard Strauss; Etude of the candidate’s choice by Kopprasch or Gallay; Orchestral Excerpts; Excerpt required of all undergraduate applicants:; Brahms – Symphony No. 3, Mvt. III (mm. 98-110) – first horn; Choose two additional excerpts from the following list:; Beethoven – Symphony No. 3, Mvt. III (trio) – second horn; Mahler – Symphony No. 1, Mvt. III (Reh. 13 to Reh. 15) – second horn; R. Strauss – Till Eulenspiegel’s Merry Pranks (opening calls) – first horn; Tchaikovsky – Symphony No. 5, Mvt. II (opening solo) – first horn; Wagner – “Short Call” from Götterdämmerung – first horn; First movement of a standard concerto of the candidate’s choice; Contrasting solo of the candidate’s choice (exposition or a selected two- to three-minute excerpt only; candidate need not prepare entire movement or piece); Excerpt required of all graduate applicants:; Richard Strauss – Ein Heldenleben (opening 16 measures) – first horn; Choose three additional excerpts from the following list:; Beethoven – Symphony No. 9, Mvt. III (mm. 82-100) – fourth horn | Audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Horn.
Exposition of the first movement of a concerto by Mozart or Richard Strauss
Etude of the candidate’s choice by Kopprasch or Gallay
Orchestral Excerpts
Excerpt required of all undergraduate applicants:
Brahms – Symphony No. 3, Mvt. III (mm. 98-110) – first horn
Choose two additional excerpts from the following list:
Beethoven – Symphony No. 3, Mvt. III (trio) – second horn
Mahler – Symphony No. 1, Mvt. III (Reh. 13 to Reh. 15) – second horn
R. Strauss – Till Eulenspiegel’s Merry Pranks (opening calls) – first horn
Tchaikovsky – Symphony No. 5, Mvt. II (opening solo) – first horn
Wagner – “Short Call” from Götterdämmerung – first horn
First movement of a standard concerto of the candidate’s choice
Contrasting solo of the candidate’s choice (exposition or a selected two- to three-minute excerpt only; candidate need not prepare entire movement or piece)
Excerpt required of all graduate applicants:
Richard Strauss – Ein Heldenleben (opening 16 measures) – first horn
Choose three additional excerpts from the following list:

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Students who have passed the pre-screening process will be asked to audition/interview at Peabody in front of a faculty panel.
Students should be prepared to discuss:
The music in their portfolio
Their musical interests in general
Why they are interested in studying at Peabody.
Applicants will also take a test in music theory called the Foundations Benchmark Exam.
Why they are interested in studying at Peabody

Students should submit work that best represents their artistic and research interests. Three tracks are available in the Computer Music Masters degree, thus each student should submit a portfolio most applicable to the track they would like to pursue:

Students interested in the **Performance Track** should prepare to perform a portfolio of 3-4 works, including some involving technology. Your performance skills will be auditioned by the Computer Music faculty.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Applicants to the MM in Musicology should submit:; Transcripts; Three letters of recommendation; An academic writing sample of approximately 1500 words, typed with footnotes and supporting scholarly research. A previously written document is acceptable.; Please provide a statement of purpose answering the following questions:; Why does musicology/ethnomusicology interest you, and what are your objectives for pursuing an MM in musicology/ethnomusicology?; Why are you interested in the program at Peabody? You can review a description of the program here, and a sample curricular plan here.; List and briefly describe the musicology/ethnomusicology courses you have taken (or are taking), as well as any major projects or papers undertaken as part of those courses. _Optional: if applicable, briefly describe other significant musicological/ethnomusicological work you may have undertaken not associated with coursework._; What would you like to work on for your thesis? _Optional: please briefly state your other areas of interest in addition to the thesis topic._; The writing sample and statement of purpose will be used as pre-screening for the interview round. Applicants who pass the pre-screening process will be invited to interview with the department faculty during audition week.; During the interview, faculty will be interested to learn more about your past work and your interest in pursuing graduate work in musicology at Peabody.; Faculty will have read your writing sample and will also have some specific questions for you about this work.

prescreen: Applicants to the MM in Musicology should submit:
Transcripts
Three letters of recommendation
An academic writing sample of approximately 1500 words, typed with footnotes and supporting scholarly research. A previously written document is acceptable.
Please provide a statement of purpose answering the following questions:
Why does musicology/ethnomusicology interest you, and what are your objectives for pursuing an MM in musicology/ethnomusicology?
Why are you interested in the program at Peabody? You can review a description of the program here, and a sample curricular plan here.

List and briefly describe the musicology/ethnomusicology courses you have taken (or are taking), as well as any major projects or papers undertaken as part of those courses. _Optional: if applicable, briefly describe other significant musicological/ethnomusicological work you may have undertaken not associated with coursework._
What would you like to work on for your thesis? _Optional: please briefly state your other areas of interest in addition to the thesis topic._

The writing sample and statement of purpose will be used as pre-screening for the interview round. Applicants who pass the pre-screening process will be invited to interview with the department faculty during audition week.
During the interview, faculty will be interested to learn more about your past work and your interest in pursuing graduate work in musicology at Peabody.
Faculty will have read your writing sample and will also have some specific questions for you about this work.

In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.; AD applicants in oboe must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.; Visit Peabody; Directory; Friedheim Music Library; Johns Hopkins University; Clery Act; Privacy; Peabody Institute of The Johns Hopkins University 1 East Mount Vernon Place Baltimore, MD 21202; 667-208-6500; Facebook; Twitter | Audition: AD applicants in oboe must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for oboe.
Undergraduate oboe auditioners should prepare:
A fast and slow Ferling etude or equivalent
One technical solo from the standard orchestral repertoire
One slow solo from a Bach cantata
Any work from the standard solo literature

The graduate repertoire in oboe is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
AD applicants in oboe must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Undergraduate; Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.; Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.; Graduate; Two arias and a song (one in English) is required. | Audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800; A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included; One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_; One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_; Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.; Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Undergraduate
Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.
Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for oboe.
Undergraduate oboe auditioners should prepare:
A fast and slow Ferling etude or equivalent
One technical solo from the standard orchestral repertoire
One slow solo from a Bach cantata
Any work from the standard solo literature

The graduate repertoire in oboe is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
AD applicants in oboe must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st
If you pass the pre-screening, you will be invited to audition with an ensemble at Peabody.
2 performance excerpts, each 8-10 minutes in length, of contrasting repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
1 rehearsal excerpt, 5 minutes total in length.
3-4 excerpts, each 2-3 minutes in length, of contrasting standard repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
A full-size orchestra is not required; a quintet with piano would suffice, for example.
Appropriate dress is expected.
Selections from the standard Wind Ensemble/Chamber Winds repertoire is preferred.
The conductor should be recorded from the front so that their eyes, face, hands, gestures and posture are all clearly seen. Please no videos filmed from the conductor’s back.
Total length of video**: 15-20 minutes recommended; 30 minutes maximum.
The subject should be shown rehearsing for part of the video and performing for the remainder.
Suggested time allocation: 1/2 rehearsal, 1/2 performance. The performance component should be just that, a complete run of a composition or movement.
The chosen excerpts should include a variety of musical styles as well as some technically challenging passages.
The conductor’s name, the repertoire, the date, and location of the selected rehearsals and performances, and the ensembles conducted.
A brief description of the type of ensemble (e.g. student, community, semi-professional, workshop, etc.)
For the rehearsal portion of the recording, please include where the recording was made in the rehearsal sequence. For example, this was the third rehearsal in a sequence of six rehearsals.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
audition: Session with a lab choir, to be conducted from the full score, _not_ a piano/vocal score
MM/GPD – Ethel Smyth / J.S. Bach: _Five Sacred Partsongs,_ No. 1 “Komm, süßer Tod” and Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_
DMA –Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_ and Stravinsky: _Symphony of Psalms,_ mvmt I
Interview/Keyboard skills assessment with Beth Willer, Director of Choral Studies
Score analysis
Regular Decision:
Schumann:_Symphony No. 2_
Expositions of all movements
Copland: _Appalachian Spring_(original 13-instrument version)
2 before #35-51
Beethoven:_Symphony No. 5_
Late Admission:
At least two contrasting excerpts from the standard repertoire
Examples include Brahms: _Symphony No. 4_, Mozart: _The Magic Flute_, Copland:_Appalachian Spring Suite_(Orchestral version)
Interview: Stravinsky, _Octet for Wind Instruments_
Audition with the Peabody Wind Ensemble: Johan de Meij,_Symphony No. 1, Mvt. 4 “Journey in the Dark”_

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Organ.
One work by J. S. Bach (except for Eight Little Preludes and Fugues)
Two contrasting works
The total time of recorded submissions for the bachelor of music should not exceed 20 minutes.
A major work by J. S. Bach (i.e. prelude and fugue, trio sonata, concerto, etc.)
Two contrasting works from French classic, 19th century German or French, or post-1910 repertoire
The total time of recorded submissions for graduate degrees or the DMA should not exceed 30 minutes.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; Etude No. 1 from Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_; Beethoven Symphony No. 9, mvt 1 coda; Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach; 5-10 minutes of a 4-mallet contemporary solo work for marimba or vibraphone (excerpts or movements from a larger work are acceptable to fit the time requirement); Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):; Druckman – _Reflections on the Nature of Water_; Etezady – _Boomslang_; Klatzow – _Dances of Earth and Fire_; Lansky – _Three Moves_; Mackey – _See Ya Thursday_; Miki – _Time for marimba_; Seo – _V, 12 Preludes for marimba_; Sueyoshi – _Mirage_; Schwantner – _Velocities_; Viñao – _Khan Variations_ | Audition: One etude from either Cirone’s _Portraits in Rhythm_ or Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_; One etude by Pratt, Wilcoxon, or similar; Etude of candidate’s choice from Richard Hochrainer _Etuden Fur Timpani_; Candidate will be expected to demonstrate fluency of tuning, and recognize and sing intervals played on the piano; Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach; Four-mallet solo (approximately 5-12 minutes in length); Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):; Abe – _Dream of the Cherry Blossoms_, _Wind in the Bamboo Grove_, _Variations on Japanese Children’s Songs_>; Miyake – _Chain_; Sammut – _Rotations_, _Cameleon_; Stout – _Mexican Dances_, _Astral Dance_; Tanaka – _Two Movements for Marimba_; Two-mallet sight-reading will be required; Excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire for timpani, snare drum, and xylophone are suggested but not required.; Two excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire; One solo from Elliott Carter’s _Eight Pieces for Four Timpani_; A major work for either marimba or vibraphone (see suggested repertoire in the pre-screening requirements above); Sight reading will be required

prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Etude No. 1 from Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_
Beethoven Symphony No. 9, mvt 1 coda
Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach
5-10 minutes of a 4-mallet contemporary solo work for marimba or vibraphone (excerpts or movements from a larger work are acceptable to fit the time requirement)
Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):
Druckman – _Reflections on the Nature of Water_
Etezady – _Boomslang_
Klatzow – _Dances of Earth and Fire_
Lansky – _Three Moves_
Mackey – _See Ya Thursday_
Miki – _Time for marimba_
Seo – _V, 12 Preludes for marimba_
Sueyoshi – _Mirage_
Schwantner – _Velocities_
Viñao – _Khan Variations_
audition: One etude from either Cirone’s _Portraits in Rhythm_ or Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_
One etude by Pratt, Wilcoxon, or similar
Etude of candidate’s choice from Richard Hochrainer _Etuden Fur Timpani_
Candidate will be expected to demonstrate fluency of tuning, and recognize and sing intervals played on the piano
Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach
Four-mallet solo (approximately 5-12 minutes in length)
Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):
Abe – _Dream of the Cherry Blossoms_, _Wind in the Bamboo Grove_, _Variations on Japanese Children’s Songs_>
Miyake – _Chain_
Sammut – _Rotations_, _Cameleon_
Stout – _Mexican Dances_, _Astral Dance_
Tanaka – _Two Movements for Marimba_
Two-mallet sight-reading will be required
Excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire for timpani, snare drum, and xylophone are suggested but not required.
Two excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire
One solo from Elliott Carter’s _Eight Pieces for Four Timpani_
A major work for either marimba or vibraphone (see suggested repertoire in the pre-screening requirements above)
Sight reading will be required

Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.; Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.; In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:; 4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.; Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.; Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.; Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.; Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

One solo from Elliott Carter’s _Eight Pieces for Four Timpani_
A major work for either marimba or vibraphone (see suggested repertoire in the pre-screening requirements above)
Sight reading will be required
