prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st; Late Admission**: May 1st; Etude No. 1 from Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_; Beethoven Symphony No. 9, mvt 1 coda; Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach; 5-10 minutes of a 4-mallet contemporary solo work for marimba or vibraphone (excerpts or movements from a larger work are acceptable to fit the time requirement); Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):; Druckman – _Reflections on the Nature of Water_; Etezady – _Boomslang_; Klatzow – _Dances of Earth and Fire_; Lansky – _Three Moves_; Mackey – _See Ya Thursday_; Miki – _Time for marimba_; Seo – _V, 12 Preludes for marimba_; Sueyoshi – _Mirage_; Schwantner – _Velocities_; Viñao – _Khan Variations_ | Audition: One etude from either Cirone’s _Portraits in Rhythm_ or Delecluse’s _Douze Etudes_; One etude by Pratt, Wilcoxon, or similar; Etude of candidate’s choice from Richard Hochrainer _Etuden Fur Timpani_; Candidate will be expected to demonstrate fluency of tuning, and recognize and sing intervals played on the piano; Two-mallet transcription for marimba of any movement of a work by J.S. Bach; Four-mallet solo (approximately 5-12 minutes in length); Suggested repertoire (you may choose from these recommendations OR a comparable piece of your choice):; Abe – _Dream of the Cherry Blossoms_, _Wind in the Bamboo Grove_, _Variations on Japanese Children’s Songs_>; Miyake – _Chain_; Sammut – _Rotations_, _Cameleon_; Stout – _Mexican Dances_, _Astral Dance_; Tanaka – _Two Movements for Marimba_; Two-mallet sight-reading will be required; Excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire for timpani, snare drum, and xylophone are suggested but not required.; Two excerpts from the standard orchestral repertoire; One solo from Elliott Carter’s _Eight Pieces for Four Timpani_; A major work for either marimba or vibraphone (see suggested repertoire in the pre-screening requirements above); Sight reading will be required

prescreen: Early Decision**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
The video must include:
A complete classical sonata
A romantic solo work of about 10 minutes or more in length. _Several shorter works can be combined, such as Brahms Op. 118, Nos. 1-3, or a movement of a large sonata (Chopin, Schumann, etc.)._
A virtuosic etude
Learn more about Pre-Screening
The following is the subsequent audition repertoire to be submitted after pre-screening or performed at a live audition:
Baroque: a prelude and fugue or any other work containing a fugue by J.S. Bach
Classical: complete sonata by Mozart, Haydn, Beethoven, or Schubert.
Beethoven’s Sonata, Op. 49 Nos 1 and 2 may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
Romantic: a major solo work by a 19th-century composer
For example, Chopin’s _Ballades, Scherzi,_ or _Sonatas;_ Brahms’_Op. 118_ or _119 (complete);_ Liszt’s _Dante_ _Sonata,_ _Vallee d’Obermann,_ or a _Hungarian Rhapsody._
A Chopin nocturne or etude may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
A representative 20 th or 21 st Century work
A virtuoso etude by one of the following composers: Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, Scriabin, Debussy, Bartok, Ligeti, or Stravinsky
TRANSFER STUDENTS ONLY: Present one additional virtuosic etude by Chopin, Liszt, Debussy, or Rachmaninoff
audition: All selections should be performed from memory.
Early Decision**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
The video must include:
A complete classical sonata
A romantic solo work of about 10 minutes or more in length. _Several shorter works can be combined, such as Brahms Op. 118, Nos. 1-3, or a movement of a large sonata (Chopin, Schumann, etc.)._

Baroque: a prelude and fugue or any other work containing a fugue by J.S. Bach
Classical: complete sonata by Mozart, Haydn, Beethoven, or Schubert.
Beethoven’s Sonata, Op. 49 Nos 1 and 2 may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
Romantic: a major solo work by a 19th-century composer
For example, Chopin’s _Ballades, Scherzi,_ or _Sonatas;_ Brahms’_Op. 118_ or _119 (complete);_ Liszt’s _Dante_ _Sonata,_ _Vallee d’Obermann,_ or a _Hungarian Rhapsody._
A Chopin nocturne or etude may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
A representative 20 th or 21 st Century work
A virtuoso etude by one of the following composers: Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, Scriabin, Debussy, Bartok, Ligeti, or Stravinsky
TRANSFER STUDENTS ONLY: Present one additional virtuosic etude by Chopin, Liszt, Debussy, or Rachmaninoff
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Repertoire includes:
A complete major romantic solo work
A video recording of a recital can be used if it was performed within the past two years.
Selections on the recording do not have to be the same ones you will perform for your audition.

A romantic solo work of about 10 minutes or more in length. _Several shorter works can be combined, such as Brahms Op. 118, Nos. 1-3, or a movement of a large sonata (Chopin, Schumann, etc.)._
A virtuosic etude
Learn more about Pre-Screening
The following is the subsequent audition repertoire to be submitted after pre-screening or performed at a live audition:
Baroque: a prelude and fugue or any other work containing a fugue by J.S. Bach
Classical: complete sonata by Mozart, Haydn, Beethoven, or Schubert.
Beethoven’s Sonata, Op. 49 Nos 1 and 2 may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
Romantic: a major solo work by a 19th-century composer
For example, Chopin’s _Ballades, Scherzi,_ or _Sonatas;_ Brahms’_Op. 118_ or _119 (complete);_ Liszt’s _Dante_ _Sonata,_ _Vallee d’Obermann,_ or a _Hungarian Rhapsody._
A Chopin nocturne or etude may **not** be used to fulfill this requirement
A representative 20 th or 21 st Century work
A virtuoso etude by one of the following composers: Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, Scriabin, Debussy, Bartok, Ligeti, or Stravinsky
TRANSFER STUDENTS ONLY: Present one additional virtuosic etude by Chopin, Liszt, Debussy, or Rachmaninoff
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Repertoire includes:
A complete major romantic solo work
A video recording of a recital can be used if it was performed within the past two years.
Selections on the recording do not have to be the same ones you will perform for your audition.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for saxophone.; Saxophone applicants should prepare the following:; Two etudes or movements from solo works demonstrating contrasting styles. Examples include, but are not limited to:; Movements of any unaccompanied works by J.S. Bach; _Star Bits_ by Corey Dundee; _48 Etudes_ by Wilhelm Ferling; _28 Etudes on Modes of Olivier Messiaen_ by Guy Lacour; _9 Etudes_ by Christian Lauba; _Vignettes_ by Remy LeBeouf; _25 Caprices_ by Sigfrid Karg-Elert; _Maï_ by Ryo Noda; _Tango Etudes_ by Astor Piazzolla; _Sonate_ by Jeanine Rueff; _Wings_ by Joan Tower; _Black Mary_ by Shelley Washington; _Naica_ by Viet Cuong; Two contrasting movements from accompanied work(s). Examples include, but are not limited to:; _Sonata, Op. 19_ by Paul Creston | Audition: AD applicants in saxophone must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three styles or historical periods, including at least two larger works, and including selections by composers from backgrounds that are under-represented in concert halls.

See additional requirements below (if any).; Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

Where, what styles, etc.; What interests you in Peabody Jazz?; If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here? | Audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.; At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:; Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:; A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.; A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.; A standard ballad.; Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).; Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.; Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.; Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.; In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:; 4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.; Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.; Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.; Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.; Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.; Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.; We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Trombone.; One lyrical étude by Bordogni, Book One; One technical étude by Kopprasch, Blazhevich, Tyrell, Voxman (or equivalent); One solo, or one movement of a concerto, of the applicant’s choice; Orchestral Excerpts are not required for Bachelor of Music auditions.; Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight.; A solo of the applicant’s choice (the complete work, or certain movements of a multi-movement work).; An etude by Bordogni/Rochut, down one octave from printed.; A technical etude of the applicant’s choice.; Examples: Blazhevich (tuba or Vernon edition), Tyrell (tuba), Grigoriev (tuba), Gillis, Maenz, Pederson, or others.; Major scales in a moderate tempo (articulated, tenuto) may be asked in order to hear sound and intonation through the applicant’s entire range.; Transfer applicants are required to prepare at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting style.; One technical étude of advanced difficulty; One complete major concerto or solo work; Three or four orchestral excerpts from the symphonic repertoire (specific excerpts to be chosen by the applicant); Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight in various clefs. ​; Please prepare the undergraduate requirements listed above, including at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting styles.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. | Audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Trombone.
One lyrical étude by Bordogni, Book One
One technical étude by Kopprasch, Blazhevich, Tyrell, Voxman (or equivalent)
One solo, or one movement of a concerto, of the applicant’s choice
Orchestral Excerpts are not required for Bachelor of Music auditions.
Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight.
A solo of the applicant’s choice (the complete work, or certain movements of a multi-movement work).
An etude by Bordogni/Rochut, down one octave from printed.
A technical etude of the applicant’s choice.
Examples: Blazhevich (tuba or Vernon edition), Tyrell (tuba), Grigoriev (tuba), Gillis, Maenz, Pederson, or others.
Major scales in a moderate tempo (articulated, tenuto) may be asked in order to hear sound and intonation through the applicant’s entire range.
Transfer applicants are required to prepare at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting style.
One technical étude of advanced difficulty
One complete major concerto or solo work
Three or four orchestral excerpts from the symphonic repertoire (specific excerpts to be chosen by the applicant)
Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight in various clefs. ​
Please prepare the undergraduate requirements listed above, including at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting styles.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.
audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

One solo, or one movement of a concerto, of the applicant’s choice; Orchestral Excerpts are not required for Bachelor of Music auditions.; Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight.; A solo of the applicant’s choice (the complete work, or certain movements of a multi-movement work).; An etude by Bordogni/Rochut, down one octave from printed.; A technical etude of the applicant’s choice.; prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Trombone.
One lyrical étude by Bordogni, Book One
One technical étude by Kopprasch, Blazhevich, Tyrell, Voxman (or equivalent)
One solo, or one movement of a concerto, of the applicant’s choice
Orchestral Excerpts are not required for Bachelor of Music auditions.
Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight.
A solo of the applicant’s choice (the complete work, or certain movements of a multi-movement work).
An etude by Bordogni/Rochut, down one octave from printed.
A technical etude of the applicant’s choice.
Examples: Blazhevich (tuba or Vernon edition), Tyrell (tuba), Grigoriev (tuba), Gillis, Maenz, Pederson, or others.
Major scales in a moderate tempo (articulated, tenuto) may be asked in order to hear sound and intonation through the applicant’s entire range.
Transfer applicants are required to prepare at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting style.
One technical étude of advanced difficulty
One complete major concerto or solo work
Three or four orchestral excerpts from the symphonic repertoire (specific excerpts to be chosen by the applicant)
Applicants may be asked to play two-octave major and minor scales and to read at sight in various clefs. ​
Please prepare the undergraduate requirements listed above, including at least three standard orchestral excerpts of contrasting styles.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Trumpet.; Two solo movements of contrasting styles from the major concerto and sonata repertoire.; Charlier Etude No. 2 Du Style; Brandt Etude No. 1, or etude of comparable difficulty level and style.; Orchestral Excerpts:; Mussorgsky: Pictures at an Exhibition – Promenade; Resphigi: Pines of Rome – offstage lyrical solo; Mahler: Symphony No. 5 – 1st movement opening solo; OR 3 excerpts from band and orchestral repertoire of your choice; Scales in all keys; possible sight reading and transposition; Haydn Trumpet Concerto – 1st Movement (complete) with Cadenza; Modern solo of your choice; Any Bitsch Etude; Mahler: Symphony No. 5 – 1st movement opening solo AND lyrical solo starting one bar before rehearsal 13; Stravinsky: Petrouchka – Ballerina Dance; Beethoven: Leonore Overture – 2 AND 3 offstage trumpet calls; Handel: Messiah Trumpet Shall Sound – full movement (on piccolo trumpet); AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works. | Audition: AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: There is no pre-screening requirement for Trumpet.
Two solo movements of contrasting styles from the major concerto and sonata repertoire.
Charlier Etude No. 2 Du Style
Brandt Etude No. 1, or etude of comparable difficulty level and style.
Orchestral Excerpts:
Mussorgsky: Pictures at an Exhibition – Promenade
Resphigi: Pines of Rome – offstage lyrical solo
Mahler: Symphony No. 5 – 1st movement opening solo
OR 3 excerpts from band and orchestral repertoire of your choice
Scales in all keys; possible sight reading and transposition
Haydn Trumpet Concerto – 1st Movement (complete) with Cadenza
Modern solo of your choice
Any Bitsch Etude
Mahler: Symphony No. 5 – 1st movement opening solo AND lyrical solo starting one bar before rehearsal 13
Stravinsky: Petrouchka – Ballerina Dance
Beethoven: Leonore Overture – 2 AND 3 offstage trumpet calls
Handel: Messiah Trumpet Shall Sound – full movement (on piccolo trumpet)

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: All applicants should prepare and submit one movement of a concerto and two movements of Bach.
Two contrasting movements of unaccompanied Bach.Please omit repeats in the dance movements.
One movement from a standard sonata (Brahms, Schubert, Schumann, Shostakovich) or a comparable work
One movement from the Bartok, Walton, or Hindemith concertos or a virtuoso movement from a standard classical concerto

The graduate repertoire in viola is patterned after a typical undergraduate senior (MM, GPD) or graduate (DMA) recital. In order to provide some latitude, the requirements are not dictated. However, you must prepare sufficient material to perform a full solo recital containing works which show a diversity of periods and styles.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods and including at least two large-scale works.

audition_requirements.repertoire_summary: Prescreen: Undergraduate; Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.; Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.; Graduate; Two arias and a song (one in English) is required. | Audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800; A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included; One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_; One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_; Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.; Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.; AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Undergraduate
Three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a foreign language.
Selections must be memorized and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Graduate
Two arias and a song (one in English) is required.
audition: 4-5 of the eight required selections must be written before 1800
A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should be included
One ricercar from Diego Ortiz:_Tratado de Glosas_
One prolusio or a set of divisions from the “Divisions for the practice of Learners” in Christopher Simpson:_The Division Viol (Part III)_
Two contrasting movements by a composer such as Abel, Marais, Schenk, Telemann, or comparable.
Applicants for graduate degrees should additionally prepare two contrasting movements by a second composer.
AD applicants must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, including at least two large-scale works.

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Of the three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a language other than English.
Selections must be memorized, and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Three pieces are required.
Please include one piece in English and one in a language other than English.
Selections should display a variety of styles and tempi.
Pieces must be memorized.
Sheet music of pieces must be presented to the provided pianist at your live audition.
A supplemental sight-singing diagnostic will be given directly after all undergraduate auditions.
Eight pieces are required.
Please include at least one English selection and at least three languages other than English.
Please show a variety of styles and include a mix of songs, operatic and/or oratorio arias, and at least one recitative.
Pieces must be memorized (except oratorio and/or complicated repertoire of the 20th/21st century).

For the MM in Historical Performance, requirements follow those of the MM in Voice, except that four or five of the eight selections ​must be written before 1800. A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should ​be included.
audition: AD applicants in voice must prepare a program of no less than 70 minutes duration, representing at least three style periods. To meet these requirements, please use the following guidelines:
Please include selections in German, French, Italian, and English
Please show a broad variety of styles which demonstrate interest in sophisticated programming, including:
at least one operatic aria
one oratorio aria
one recitative which connects to one of the above arias or that is freestanding
other non-operatic pieces
Must be memorized (except oratorio and/or complicated repertoire of the 21st century)
Please include one selection composed since 1990
Please submit a hypothetical (or actual) well-conceived recital program
Additional selections may be added to demonstrate repertoire and/or additional language passions

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
First piece must demonstrate a medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
Second piece must demonstrate a standard ballad _or_ a medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
Scales: choose one flat key _and_ one sharp key to demonstrate the following:
Two octave major scale
Two octave melodic minor _ascending only_ (do not descend with natural minor)
Two octave Dorian minor
Two octave harmonic minor
Each of the corresponding arpeggios
Provide three recorded examples of your original compositions and the corresponding scores or charts.
Please also include a brief video/clip of you answering the following questions and speaking about your jazz background:
How long have you studied jazz? Where, what styles, etc.
What interests you in Peabody Jazz?
If you were to attend Peabody, what would you expect to learn while here?
audition: All applicants who advance to the audition round will be required to complete an online theory assessment known as “Foundations Benchmark” during the month of January.
At the live auditions, a rhythm section comprised of current students will provide live accompaniment. The audition comprises performances of:
Performance (melody and improvisation) of three tunes from the standard jazz repertoire:
A medium tempo 12-bar jazz blues.
A medium or medium up-tempo standard jazz tune.
A standard ballad.
Comping is required as part of the above repertoire for piano, bass, drums, and guitar. See additional requirements below (if any).
Drummers must demonstrate both the ability to delineate the melody and must solo over the form of each of the required vehicles. See additional information below.

Improvisation will be assessed by observing the musicians ability to demonstrate knowledge of the chord progressions of their selected pieces through their melodic choices on the chord progression. Harmonic, rhythmic and style accuracy will determine one’s ability to be successful in the program.
Standards should be selected from the songbooks of Ellington, Rodgers & Hart, Berlin, Porter, Gershwin, Kern, etc. Vocal applicants with piano skills are encouraged to accompany themselves.
In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

In addition to all other requirements, bass players must:

4/4 walking, a slow, medium or fast 12 bar blues, medium or fast ‘rhythm changes’, a tune form such as Groovin’ High, Four, There’ll Never Be Another You, Confirmation, Out of Nowhere, or Autumn Leaves.
Jazz waltz time – a tune like Someday my Prince will Come, Up Jumped Spring, All Blues, or Blusette.

Slow Ballad accompaniment – A standard ballad form, such as Body and Soul, What’s New, In a Sentimental Mood, Soul Eyes, or Sophisticated Lady. Be prepared to play a tune of this type in a slow ‘2’, or half note pulse, without going into a 4/4 walking line.

Ostinato pattern – This could be a part of a tune that has a repeating bassline such as Bolivia, or Joshua, the intro ‘vamp’ from Song for my Father, or the opening bassline in Footprints. It’s not difficult to find ostinatos within the standard jazz repertoire. Just pick one that you like.

Play something that demonstrates the level of technical proficiency. This could be an etude or technical study from a bass method or etude book, a scale study, or a solo transcription. Something that you’ve work on, and fell that you can play well.

Double Bassists only**– Play a melody of your choice with the bow. This can be included as part of your required jazz audition tunes. Some good choices might be Body and Soul, What’s New, Goodbye Pork Pie Hat, or In a Sentimental Mood.

We won’t have time to hear all of these during your audition, but you should be prepared to play an excerpt of each one. They can be short excerpts, but in each case should demonstrate that you understand what it is that we’re asking you to do. If you’re unfamiliar with the styles, songs, or terminology that we’ve used here, please go to one of the thousands of jazz resources out there and look them up. Learning to be resourceful in your studies is an essential part of your jazz education, and you need to begin...

prescreen: Early Decision (Undergraduate only)**: November 1st
Spring Admission (MM, GPD only)**: with your application
Regular Decision**: December 1st
Late Admission**: May 1st
Of the three classical selections required, at least one should be in English and one in a language other than English.
Selections must be memorized, and should show a variety of styles and tempi.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
Three pieces are required.
Please include one piece in English and one in a language other than English.
Selections should display a variety of styles and tempi.
Pieces must be memorized.
Sheet music of pieces must be presented to the provided pianist at your live audition.
A supplemental sight-singing diagnostic will be given directly after all undergraduate auditions.
Eight pieces are required.
Please include at least one English selection and at least three languages other than English.
Please show a variety of styles and include a mix of songs, operatic and/or oratorio arias, and at least one recitative.
Pieces must be memorized (except oratorio and/or complicated repertoire of the 20th/21st century).

For the MM in Historical Performance, requirements follow those of the MM in Voice, except that four or five of the eight selections ​must be written before 1800. A Gregorian chant and at least one selection from the Renaissance should ​be included.

prescreen: Regular Decision**: December 1st
If you pass the pre-screening, you will be invited to audition with an ensemble at Peabody.
2 performance excerpts, each 8-10 minutes in length, of contrasting repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
1 rehearsal excerpt, 5 minutes total in length.
3-4 excerpts, each 2-3 minutes in length, of contrasting standard repertoire demonstrating a variety of styles and tempi.
A full-size orchestra is not required; a quintet with piano would suffice, for example.
Appropriate dress is expected.
Selections from the standard Wind Ensemble/Chamber Winds repertoire is preferred.
The conductor should be recorded from the front so that their eyes, face, hands, gestures and posture are all clearly seen. Please no videos filmed from the conductor’s back.
Total length of video**: 15-20 minutes recommended; 30 minutes maximum.
The subject should be shown rehearsing for part of the video and performing for the remainder.
Suggested time allocation: 1/2 rehearsal, 1/2 performance. The performance component should be just that, a complete run of a composition or movement.
The chosen excerpts should include a variety of musical styles as well as some technically challenging passages.
The conductor’s name, the repertoire, the date, and location of the selected rehearsals and performances, and the ensembles conducted.
A brief description of the type of ensemble (e.g. student, community, semi-professional, workshop, etc.)
For the rehearsal portion of the recording, please include where the recording was made in the rehearsal sequence. For example, this was the third rehearsal in a sequence of six rehearsals.
Learn more about Pre-Screening
audition: Session with a lab choir, to be conducted from the full score, _not_ a piano/vocal score
MM/GPD – Ethel Smyth / J.S. Bach: _Five Sacred Partsongs,_ No. 1 “Komm, süßer Tod” and Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_
DMA –Jonathan Dove: “Ring Out Wild Bells,” from _The Passing of the Year_ and Stravinsky: _Symphony of Psalms,_ mvmt I
Interview/Keyboard skills assessment with Beth Willer, Director of Choral Studies
Score analysis
Regular Decision:
Schumann:_Symphony No. 2_
Expositions of all movements
Copland: _Appalachian Spring_(original 13-instrument version)
2 before #35-51
Beethoven:_Symphony No. 5_
Late Admission:
At least two contrasting excerpts from the standard repertoire
Examples include Brahms: _Symphony No. 4_, Mozart: _The Magic Flute_, Copland:_Appalachian Spring Suite_(Orchestral version)
Interview: Stravinsky, _Octet for Wind Instruments_
Audition with the Peabody Wind Ensemble: Johan de Meij,_Symphony No. 1, Mvt. 4 “Journey in the Dark”_
